calendar.matters:
(Amended) Ntc Of Hearing For Writ Of Mandate
Off Calendar Mar-02-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the tentative ruling is adopted on PETITIONER ANNA THADDEA's (Amended) Notice Of Hearing For Writ Of Mandate, as follows:

Petitioner Thaddea's petition for administrative mandate is denied. The court employs the independent judgment test when reviewing respondent's decision. (See Lake v. Reed (1997) 16 Cal.4th 448, 456.) "[I]n exercising its independent judgment, a trial court must afford a strong presumption of correctness concerning the administrative findings, and the party challenging the administrative decision bears the burden of convincing the court that the administrative findings are contrary to the weight of the evidence." (Fukuda v. City of Angels (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 817.)

In this case, petitioner has not met her burden and the court concludes that respondent did not abuse its discretion. Petitioner was properly advised of the consequences of refusing or failing to complete the BAC test. (DS 367 (Chemical Test Admonition).) Petitioner in fact failed and/or refused to complete the chemical test after being admonished. She did not exhale during the breath test and that rendered it invalid. (Incident Report, pg. 5.) Petitioner was obligated to complete the test, however. (See Cole v. Dep't of Motor Vehicles (1983) 139 Cal.App.3d 870, 875.) Petitioner also refused to consent to a blood test and requested a lawyer and her dog. (DS 367 (Chemical Test Admonition).) (See People v. Bury (1996) 41 Cal.App.4th 1194, 1200, n.3 [conditional agreement to consent to test constitutes a refusal].)

The officers' sworn statements support respondent's decision. Petitioner failed to rebut the presumption that the officers accurately observed and reported the facts. (Evid. Code, 664.) Petitioner complains that her attorney did not properly advise her regarding the administrative hearing. Petitioner did not have an absolute right to appear in person. (See Arnett v. Office of Admin. Hearings (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 332, 340, n.4.) Petitioner waived her ability to be personally present at the hearing by appearing through counsel. Petitioner's counsel's failure to advise her regarding the details of her hearing is a matter between petitioner and her counsel, it is not a basis to vacate respondent's decision. There is no right to effective counsel in an administrative disciplinary proceeding. (See White v. Board of Med. Quality Assur. (1982) 128 Cal.App.3d 699, 707.)

Petitioner's complaint that respondent's decision was mailed to the wrong address constitutes harmless error. The ineffective notice only relates to the decision and petitioner filed the instant petition in a timely fashion and is receiving a Superior Court hearing on the merits.

In the underlying hearing, petitioner's counsel argued that respondent could not revoke petitioner's license because there was insufficient evidence to show that petitioner was driving while intoxicated. Petitioner does not raise that argument in this proceeding. Nonetheless, the court concludes that there was sufficient evidence to support that finding given petitioner's admitted inebriation, her improper parking job, and the whiskey bottles located in her car. (Incident Report, pgs. 4-5.)

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

(Amended) Ntc Of Hearing For Writ Of Mandate
